summary:
The Yarmukian culture was a Pottery Neolithic culture of the southern Levant, centered in the Jordan Valley and extending into parts of modern Israel, Jordan, and Syria, dating from approximately 6400–6000 BCE. Named after the Yarmouk River where characteristic sites were first identified, the culture is defined by a distinctive coarse pottery decorated with incised herringbone patterns and filled with a red wash, as well as by small stone and clay figurines often referred to as the Yarmukian mother goddess.

Yarmukian communities practiced mixed farming, combining cereal cultivation with animal husbandry, and were organized in small to medium-sized villages. The figurines produced by this culture represent some of the most stylized anthropomorphic art of the Levantine Neolithic, with geometric renderings of the human form that depart significantly from the more naturalistic figures of contemporary Anatolian traditions. The Yarmukian culture is considered part of the broad Pottery Neolithic horizon that transformed the Levant following the Pre-Pottery Neolithic period.
